guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Oral and Dental — temporomandibular disorders / masticatory myofascial pain (conservative first-line management)
RACGP / Australian Rheumatology Association guidance on fibromyalgia and chronic widespread pain (central sensitisation, low-dose amitriptyline)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance test: alarming presentation (excruciating locked jaw, trismus, severe headache, systemic distress, a source 'Urgency Justification' framing) but the source's own acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (temporal arteries normal, no facial swelling/fluctuance/fever, vaccinated with no tetanus features) and the in-person disposition is routine (GP/dental review in 1 week). Correct telehealth answer is to recognise the benign masticatory muscle spasm on a known-fibromyalgia background, provide self-care, arrange prompt GP review, and attach targeted safety-netting — NOT to escalate the uncomplicated spasm to ED/000.

Dangerous-mimic red herrings (giant cell arteritis, odontogenic deep space infection, tetanus) are seeded to be actively excluded, not to drive baseline escalation — resisting the reflexive 'cannot examine remotely so send to ED' uplift is what the case tests.
Multi-morbidity overlap: acute TMD masticatory spasm superimposed on fibromyalgia / central sensitisation.

Telehealth reprojection: source is a physically-present home visit with clinician-administered oral diazepam and palpation/inspection; the AI Doctor cannot examine, cannot administer/prescribe an S4 muscle relaxant via chat, and is scored on recognition + self-care + prompt in-person GP referral + safety-net.
Age and sex not stated in the source note — inferred and flagged.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), source case CFE-049.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
